common_name: Lakota Squash (Native American Heirloom Winter Squash)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil reaches 65°F; Lakota is a striking Native American heirloom winter squash with vivid deep orange skin dramatically marked with irregular dark green and black mottling — one of the most visually beautiful and historically significant native food squashes, producing teardrop-shaped fruits of 5–8 pounds with exceptionally sweet, rich, deeply colored orange flesh.

directions: Direct sow in hills of 3 seeds (thin to 2) after soil warms; provide 4–8 feet of vine space; harvest when the skin hardens completely and can no longer be dented with a fingernail, and the stem begins to dry and cork; cure at room temperature for 10–14 days after harvest to develop optimal flavor and maximize storage life. Lakota squash is an heirloom winter squash associated with the Lakota (Sioux) people of the Great Plains, one of the native peoples of North America who cultivated squash as a primary food staple long before European contact. The variety produces one of the most visually distinctive squashes in cultivation: teardrop or pear-shaped fruits with deep orange-red skin boldly marked with irregular splotches and streaks of dark green and near-black — a coloration so striking that Lakota is as ornamental as it is edible. The flesh is the intensely orange, dry, sweet, and finely textured type characteristic of the best Cucurbita maxima varieties: rich enough to need nothing added beyond butter and salt, sweet enough to serve as dessert, and dry enough to bake or purée without becoming watery. The flavor is often compared favorably to kabocha and red kuri squash — comparable sweetness and dryness but with a distinctive terroir-like richness that differentiates it from commercial varieties. Lakota stores exceptionally well — 4–6 months under cool, dry conditions — making it a true long-keeping winter provision. Average fruits weigh 5–8 lbs with moderate vine length suitable for home gardens.

common_name: Sunshine Kabocha Squash (AAS-Winning Orange Kabocha-Type Winter Squash)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost; Sunshine is an All-America Selections award-winning (2004) kabocha-type winter squash producing compact 3–4 lb fruits with brilliant orange skin and intensely sweet, rich, dry orange flesh — the classic kabocha-style eating quality in a smaller, more manageable fruit size than traditional green Japanese kabocha varieties.

directions: Direct sow 1 inch deep after last frost in a warm, well-prepared bed; Sunshine squash vines are moderately vigorous and less sprawling than giant winter squash types; harvest when the stem corks and the skin is uniformly bright orange and resists fingernail denting; flavor improves significantly with 2–4 weeks of curing after harvest. Sunshine is a kabocha-type F1 hybrid winter squash that won the All-America Selections award in 2004 for outstanding garden performance — its combination of attractive appearance, reliable productivity, and outstanding eating quality made it an immediate success with home gardeners across the country. The squash belongs to the kabocha type (Cucurbita maxima), an East Asian tradition of winter squash with dry, dense, intensely sweet flesh that is considered the finest eating quality among all winter squash types. While traditional Japanese kabocha cultivars have dark green or grey skin with pale stripes, Sunshine was selected for brilliant orange skin that is instantly attractive and visually distinctive in the garden and at market. The flesh is the characteristic kabocha type: intensely deep orange, very dry, sweet, and starchy — so dense and sweet that it bakes without becoming watery, purées into thick soups without thickening, and can be used in dessert preparations. The compact 3–4 pound fruit size is practical for household use (one squash makes 2–4 servings without left-over waste) and the moderate vine size makes it manageable in home gardens with less space than butternut or hubbard types. Excellent storage for 3–4 months under cool conditions.

common_name: Poona Kheera Cucumber (Indian Heirloom Color-Changing Cucumber)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost; Poona Kheera is an Indian heirloom cucumber that dramatically changes color as it matures — from white to pale yellow to apple-yellow and finally to russet-tan — and is prized for its exceptional flavor at the yellow stage and above, good heat tolerance, and thin, easily digestible skin; one of the most beloved traditional cucumber varieties in the Pune (Poona) region of Maharashtra, India.

directions: Direct sow 1 inch deep and provide a trellis for best production; harvest at any stage from white (mild, crisp) to apple-yellow (sweetest, most complex) to russet (mellow, slightly sweet and aromatic); the yellow-to-russet stage is the traditional eating stage in India but many American gardeners prefer the pale yellow stage for fresh salads. Poona Kheera is an heirloom cucumber from the Pune (formerly Poona) region of Maharashtra state in western India, where it has been grown for centuries as the primary cucumber of the region and is still widely available in Indian markets. The most distinctive characteristic of this variety is its remarkable color change through the maturation cycle: young fruits are white or pale ivory, then transition to pale yellow, apple-yellow, golden-yellow, and finally to a warm russet-tan at full maturity — no other commonly grown cucumber goes through such a dramatic color progression. The flavor also changes with color: white fruits are mild, crisp, and relatively bland; yellow fruits have developed characteristic cucumber fragrance, sweetness, and complexity; russet fruits have an almost apple-like sweetness and aroma distinct from young cucumber flavor. In the Indian culinary tradition, the yellow-to-russet stage is preferred for eating raw out of hand, in raitas, and for cooking; American gardeners often prefer the yellow stage for salads where the flavor is sweet and the flesh remains crisp. Poona Kheera has good heat tolerance compared to standard slicing cucumbers and adapts well to Gulf Coast and Southern growing conditions.

common_name: Gem Squash (South African Baby Round Squash, Bush Squash)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost; Gem squash is a South African heirloom summer squash producing compact, round, dark green fruits the size of a tennis ball to softball (3–4 inches diameter) that are harvested at a small, tender stage unlike other summer squashes — the immature Gem squash is cooked whole or halved rather than sliced, and is the most widely grown squash in home gardens throughout South Africa.

directions: Direct sow 1 inch deep after last frost; Gem squash grows as a compact bush (unlike most squash vines) and is excellent for small gardens and containers; harvest when fruits reach 3–4 inches (tennis-ball to softball size) with dark green skin — do not allow to mature to yellow or tan as flavor and texture degrade; cook whole (boiled, steamed, or baked) until just tender. Gem squash is a South African heirloom variety with deep roots in the home gardens of South Africa, where it is so culturally central that it appears in traditional Afrikaner recipes, South African school cookbooks, and family food traditions going back to the early colonial period. The small, round, dark green fruits are harvested at a much smaller stage than American summer squash — when they are about the size of a tennis ball to softball, with soft seeds and tender flesh — and are traditionally cooked whole rather than sliced. The most common preparation in South Africa involves boiling or steaming the whole small squash in salted water until tender, then halving and scooping out the seeded core, filling the cavity with butter, cream cheese, or condensed milk (a classic dessert preparation), and serving the squash halves on the plate. The flavor of properly young Gem squash is milder and sweeter than zucchini, with a delicate texture that makes it very versatile. The bush-type plant (not a vine) is exceptionally productive, producing many small fruits from a compact plant, making it practical for small raised-bed gardens. Increasingly available in American specialty and heirloom seed catalogs as gardeners discover this productive, compact, and culturally interesting vegetable.

common_name: Ambercup Squash (Golden Orange Compact Kabocha-Style Winter Squash)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil reaches 65°F; Ambercup is a kabocha-type winter squash with golden-amber to deep orange skin and intensely rich, sweet, dry flesh — one of the best-flavored compact winter squashes for home gardens, producing 5–7 lb fruits with firm, dense flesh that stores well through winter; similar to Red Kuri in texture and sweetness but with a distinctive golden-orange skin.

directions: Direct sow 1 inch deep after last frost in well-drained, fertile soil; vines are moderate in length (6–8 feet), more manageable than butternut or hubbard types; harvest when the skin is a uniform deep golden-amber and resists fingernail denting; cure for 2–3 weeks after harvest to maximize sweetness and storage life. Ambercup is an heirloom-type open-pollinated winter squash of the Cucurbita maxima species — the same species as kabocha, Red Kuri, Blue Hubbard, and Buttercup — that produces compact, round to slightly flattened fruits with smooth, glossy, deep golden-orange skin and a very small indented cavity at the blossom end. The flesh is the dry, dense, intensely orange, and very sweet type characteristic of the best maxima squashes, making Ambercup one of the most flavorful winter squashes for baking, roasting, and soup-making. The golden-amber skin is distinctive and attractive — warmer in color than the grey-green of standard kabocha, lighter than Buttercup's dark green, and different from Red Kuri's red-orange — making it visually distinctive in the garden and at market. Ambercup performs reliably across a wide range of climates from zone 3 to zone 9 and stores exceptionally well under cool, dry conditions — quality often improves with 1–2 months of storage as the starches convert to sugars. In zone 9a and the Gulf South, Ambercup should be direct-sown in late March or early April to allow the full season before summer heat arrives.
